description:
You are working as an MLOps engineer who maintains a small, local
    experiment-tracking workspace located under /home/user/experiments.  
    A colleague has already dropped a comma-separated metrics file here:

        /home/user/experiments/metrics.csv

    The file contains a header row followed by exactly three data rows,
    one per experiment run.  Each line has the following layout:

        run_id,accuracy,loss

    Example (do **not** introduce any extra spaces):

        run_001,0.87,0.34
        run_002,0.91,0.29
        run_003,0.89,0.31

    Task
    ----
    1. Read /home/user/experiments/metrics.csv, identify the **single**
       run whose “accuracy” value is the highest.
    2. Create the directory /home/user/experiments/reports (if it does
       not already exist).
    3. Inside that directory, create a JSON file named
       /home/user/experiments/reports/best_run.json that contains a
       compact, one-line JSON object with the three fields shown below
       in exactly this order **and with no trailing newline**:

       {"run_id":"<RUN_ID>","accuracy":<ACCURACY>,"loss":<LOSS>}

       • <RUN_ID>   – the run_id from the row with the highest accuracy  
       • <ACCURACY> – the accuracy value written as a number (no quotes)  
       • <LOSS>     – the corresponding loss value written as a number

       Using the example numbers above, the final file would look
       exactly like this (one line, no spaces other than the single
       commas that separate values):

       {"run_id":"run_002","accuracy":0.91,"loss":0.29}

       Your solution will be automatically checked for:
       • correct filename and path  
       • JSON must be one single line, no extra whitespace or newline  
       • correct field order and precise numeric values

    4. For reproducibility, also create a plain-text log file named
       /home/user/experiments/reports/extract.log that records, **one
       command per line**, every shell command you executed to perform
       this extraction (maximum 4 lines, matching the real commands you
       used; do not include comments or command output).

    Notes
    -----
    • You do not have root privileges; keep everything inside /home/user.  
    • Use any standard GNU/Linux utilities available in a typical
      container (e.g., cat, awk, csvtool, sort, jq, etc.).  
    • Do **not** hard-code the answer: always base it on the actual CSV
      content present in the file.  
    • Make sure both files are world-readable (default 0644 is fine).

    When you are finished, you should have exactly two new files:

        /home/user/experiments/reports/best_run.json
        /home/user/experiments/reports/extract.log
truth:
Initial setup before the agent starts:
      1. Directory /home/user/experiments already exists.
      2. /home/user/experiments/metrics.csv exists with the following
         exact contents, including header row and terminating newlines:

         run_id,accuracy,loss
         run_001,0.87,0.34
         run_002,0.91,0.29
         run_003,0.89,0.31

    Expected artefacts after successful task completion:

      • /home/user/experiments/reports/best_run.json
          Content (single line, **no final newline character**):
          {"run_id":"run_002","accuracy":0.91,"loss":0.29}

      • /home/user/experiments/reports/extract.log
          Content: between 1 and 4 lines, each line containing a
          shell command actually typed by the agent.  These commands
          may vary, but the file MUST end with a newline character.

          The automated checker will only validate that:
            – the file exists,
            – it is non-empty,
            – it has at most 4 newline-separated lines.

      All other filesystem state remains unchanged.
